medical.title: Researchers show retinal images can accurately differentiate ALS and Alzheimer's
medical.summary: A retinal image could help doctors quickly distinguish between similar neurodegenerative diseases, such as ALS and Alzheimer's disease, and with remarkable accuracy, according to new research published in the journal Alzheimer's & Dementia. There is currently no objective diagnostic test for ALS or Frontotemporal Lobular Dementia (FTLD-TDP), in which the protein TDP-43 forms deposits in the spinal cord and brain, respectively.

medical.title: Is vaping more harmful than smoking cigarettes? More people incorrectly think so
medical.summary: A growing number of U.S. adults consider electronic cigarettes (e-cigarettes) more harmful than conventional cigarettes. The findings, by researchers from the UT Southwestern Medical Center and published in Nicotine and Tobacco Research, could have significant implications for public health policymakers, tobacco control strategies, and people who use either of these cigarette types.

medical.title: In former college athletes, more concussions associated with worse brain health
medical.summary: In former college athletes, having had three or more concussions was associated with slightly worse physical, mental, behavioral and cognitive health five years after graduation, according to an article published March 11, 2026, in Neurology, the medical journal of the American Academy of Neurology.

medical.title: Neurologists issue guidance on the use of wearable devices
medical.summary: Wearable devices and digital apps that are available to consumers are emerging as potential tools in neurological care. These include smart watches and fitness trackers to monitor heart rate, physical activity, sleep, and other health factors. To provide neurologists with guidance on the use, limitations, and evidence on non-U.S. Food & Drug Administration (FDA)-cleared wearable technology, the American Academy of Neurology has developed an Emerging Issues in Neurology article published March 11, 2026, in Neurology.

medical.title: Why street layouts may matter for aging brains: Brain scans link walkability to memory
medical.summary: The simple act of crossing a road could help shield the brain from dementia and other cognitive conditions, according to new research from the Australian Catholic University and UNSW Sydney's Center for Healthy Brain Aging (CHeBA). The study found that people who lived in cities and frequently crossed intersections or relied on complex navigational skills had larger hippocampal tails.

medical.title: New cancer therapies target epigenetic switch
medical.summary: Cancers emerge from many different paths. One path begins early, in embryonic development, when a protein complex called PRC2, which regulates cell differentiation, identity, and plasticity, becomes dysfunctional. PRC2 has well-established links to breast, prostate, blood, and skin cancers, among others.

medical.title: One in three parents believe children always need antibiotics for ear infections
medical.summary: Over a third (36%) of surveyed parents believe children always need antibiotics for ear infections, according to new research appearing in BJGP Open. The study is titled "A survey of parental health-seeking behaviour, knowledge, and expectations around ear infection symptoms in children."

medical.title: People with HIV did not show more severe clinical symptoms during the 2022 mpox outbreak in Spain
medical.summary: People affected by human immunodeficiency virus (HIV), which causes AIDS, did not develop more severe forms of mpox than HIV-negative people during the multiregional outbreak of this disease that occurred in Spain in 2022. This is one of the main conclusions of an article published in Scientific Reports, which has analyzed 1,158 confirmed cases of mpox in adult men. The cases were reported between June 2022 and January 2023 in seven autonomous communities. The population sample analyzed in the study represents more than half of the cases recorded in Spain during this period.

medical.title: Robots vs. therapists: Live experiment tests AI's ability to give relationship advice
medical.summary: Can artificial intelligence offer meaningful relationship advice, or do human therapists still provide something machines cannot replicate? Researchers from the Max Planck Institute for Psycholinguistics recently explored this question during a public experiment at the InScience Film Festival in the city of Nijmegen.

medical.title: Combining exercise and sleep coaching can help improve sleep and health markers in young women
medical.summary: Instances of insomnia are on the rise globally, which is a matter of great concern, as lack of sleep can take a heavy toll on one's physical and mental health. A recent study by The Education University of Hong Kong investigated how changes in daily habits, such as exercise and a sleep health intervention, can affect sleep duration and quality in adults under 30.

medical.title: Out-of-hospital cardiac arrest incidence rises on postholiday weekdays
medical.summary: In South Korea, postholiday weekdays are associated with a significantly increased incidence of out-of-hospital cardiac arrest, according to a study published online March 6 in JAMA Network Open.

medical.title: Promoting physical activity can be surprisingly inexpensive, yet impacts are rarely measured
medical.summary: A joint study by the University of Oulu and the Department of Sports and Exercise Medicine, Oulu Deaconess Institute Foundation highlights the need for better evaluation of both the effectiveness and the costs of interventions designed to increase physical activity. The study is an international systematic review examining the effectiveness and cost-effectiveness of population-level physical activity interventions.

medical.title: Active ingredient of Viagra can help treat rare genetic disease
medical.summary: Sildenafil—an active ingredient also marketed under the name of Viagra—improves symptoms in patients with Leigh syndrome. This has now been reported in the journal Cell by researchers at Charité—Universitätsmedizin Berlin, together with teams from Heinrich Heine University Düsseldorf (HHU), University Hospital Düsseldorf (UKD), and the Fraunhofer Institute for Translational Medicine and Pharmacology ITMP in Hamburg. In a pilot study involving six patients, sildenafil showed a positive effect on the course of the disease.

medical.title: FDA approves Sotyktu for adults with active psoriatic arthritis
medical.summary: The U.S. Food and Drug Administration has approved Sotyktu (deucravacitinib) for the treatment of adults with active psoriatic arthritis.

medical.title: Epstein-Barr virus antibodies can distinguish MS from other neuroinflammatory diseases
medical.summary: The connection between multiple sclerosis and Epstein-Barr virus (EBV) is strengthening, according to a paper published this week in JAMA Neurology by a team of international researchers, including one from the University at Buffalo. Bianca Weinstock-Guttman, MD, SUNY Distinguished Professor in the Department of Neurology at the Jacobs School of Medicine and Biomedical Sciences at UB, a leading international expert on MS and the director of the New York State MS Consortium, is one of the co-authors of the paper titled "Epstein-Barr Virus Antibodies to Differentiate Multiple Sclerosis From Other Neuroinflammatory Diseases."

medical.title: Previously hidden immune circuit in the uterus sheds light on preeclampsia and early pregnancy failure
medical.summary: Early pregnancy depends on a remarkable act of coordination. Before the placenta can nourish a growing fetus, the embryo must securely "land" and connect with the mother's blood supply—a process guided by a specialized group of immune cells called uterine natural killer cells, or uNK cells.

medical.title: Medicinal cannabis eases endometriosis, pelvic pain
medical.summary: Medicinal cannabis could provide a new treatment option for those with endometriosis, with a study led by the University of Otago, Wellington showing it reduced pain, improved sleep and lowered anxiety. The study, led by Dr. Claire Henry from the Department of Surgery and Critical Care, involved 28 people with endometriosis and/or related pelvic pain who were prescribed cannabidiol (CBD) oil alone or in combination with dried cannabis flower for three months.

medical.title: Turning cancer's protein machinery against itself to boost immunity
medical.summary: A new study led by Pierre Close's team (GIGA, Laboratory of Cancer Signaling, and WELRI Investigator) reveals how subtly disrupting the way tumors produce their proteins can trigger a potent antitumor immune response. Researchers from the University of Liège and international collaborators have discovered an unexpected way to stimulate the immune system against cancer: by subtly disrupting how tumor cells manufacture their proteins.

medical.title: 16-year study finds major health burden in single‑ventricle heart disease
medical.summary: Children born with single-ventricle heart disease, a rare and serious heart defect, often undergo multiple surgeries in their first years of life. A new study shows the challenges for these children can last well into adolescence and sometimes throughout their lives.

medical.title: Orchestrated multi-agent AI systems outperform single agents in health care
medical.summary: As artificial intelligence (AI) becomes more common in health care, from managing records to assisting with medication decisions, researchers at the Icahn School of Medicine at Mount Sinai are asking an important question: How well does AI hold up when the workload gets intense at a health system scale?

science.title: Golden lancehead genome reveals how genes responsible for venom toxins evolved
science.summary: A research team led by scientists at the Butantan Institute in São Paulo, Brazil, has completed the most extensive genetic sequencing of a jararaca viper to date. The focus of the study was the genome of the golden lancehead (Bothrops insularis), particularly its venom genes. Since the species shares most of its genes with the other 48 species in the genus, the data serve as a reference for broader studies on the evolution of jararaca vipers and their toxins. The study is published in the journal Genome Biology and Evolution.

science.title: Heavy water expands energy potential of carbon nanotube yarns
science.summary: Researchers at The University of Texas at Dallas have developed a new electrolyte system that significantly boosts the energy-harvesting performance of twistrons, which are carbon nanotube yarns that generate electricity when repeatedly stretched. The findings could aid in the manufacturing of intelligent textiles, such as fabrics used to make spacesuits, that would power wearable electronic devices or sensors by harvesting energy from human motion.

science.title: Entangled molecules can capture sulfate, offering future solutions for water purification and environmental monitoring
science.summary: Researchers at the University of Jyväskylä (Finland) have developed a new class of synthetic molecules that can capture sulfate, a widespread industrial and environmental contaminant, with unprecedented efficiency in water. The study demonstrates that entangled molecular structures, long considered mainly chemical curiosities, can be deliberately engineered for real-world applications, including water purification, chemical sensing, and environmental monitoring. The study is published in the journal Chem.

science.title: Titanium complexes cleanly edit the core skeleton of highly stable organic compounds
science.summary: Multi-titanium hydrides can selectively snip the strong structural bonds of stable organic molecules called pyridines, RIKEN researchers have shown. This discovery could guide designing catalysts for applications in multiple branches of industrial chemistry, from oil refining to the synthesis of functional organic molecules. The findings are published in the Journal of the American Chemical Society.

science.title: How climate-friendly is insect protein really?
science.summary: The larvae of the black soldier fly (Hermetia illucens) are considered a promising source of sustainable protein, as they contain high-quality protein comparable to soy protein. They can produce this protein from a wide variety of biomass sources, including by-products and residues from agriculture and food processing. However, while their potential is the subject of intense debate, little is known about the emission of greenhouse gases during their rearing.

science.title: Compact vacuum ultraviolet laser may improve nanotechnology and power nuclear clocks
science.summary: Physicists at the University of Colorado Boulder have demonstrated a new kind of vacuum ultraviolet laser that is 100 to 1,000 times more efficient than existing technologies of its kind. The researchers say the device could one day allow scientists to observe phenomena currently out of reach for even the most powerful microscopes—such as following fuel molecules in real time as they undergo combustion, spotting incredibly small defects in nanoelectronics and more.

science.title: Galaxy-group motion suggests slower expansion in our cosmic neighborhood
science.summary: Two new studies have measured the expansion of the universe in our immediate cosmic neighborhood using a novel method that analyzes the motion of two nearby galaxy groups within their surrounding cosmic flow. The results indicate that the local universe is expanding more slowly than previously estimated, bringing measurements of nearby galaxies into close agreement with observations of the early universe. The findings also suggest that less dark matter is required to explain the dynamics of galaxies within these groups than previously assumed.

science.title: Astronomers collect rare evidence of two planets colliding
science.summary: Anastasios (Andy) Tzanidakis was combing through old telescope data from 2020 when he found an otherwise boring star acting very strangely. The star, named Gaia20ehk, was about 11,000 light-years from Earth near the constellation Puppis. It was a stable "main sequence" star, much like our sun, which meant that it should emit steady, predictable light. Yet this star began to flicker wildly.

science.title: Friendly fungi hijack plant regulator to promote symbiosis
science.summary: Plants are constantly on guard. Their roots are equipped with molecular alarm systems that detect invading microbes and trigger immune responses. Yet beneficial soil fungi routinely enter living root cells and establish close partnerships essential for plant nutrition. Scientists have now discovered that these fungi send small RNAs into the plant that may quietly switch off selected defense systems from within.

science.title: New method reveals hidden stereochemical forms of oxidation in antibody drugs
science.summary: Monoclonal antibodies are among the most widely used biologic medicines, with applications ranging from cancer treatment to autoimmune disease therapy. However, during manufacturing, storage, or transport, antibodies can undergo chemical changes that affect their stability and function. One common modification is oxidation of the amino acid methionine.

science.title: Twisted bilayer photonic crystals dynamically tune light's handedness
science.summary: Researchers at the Harvard John A. Paulson School of Engineering and Applied Sciences (SEAS) have created a chip-scale device that can dynamically control the "handedness" of light as it passes through—also known as its optical chirality—with a simple twist of two specially designed photonic crystals. The study is published in the journal Optica.

science.title: Bromacker deposit is 4 million years older than previously thought, new dating reveals
science.summary: Analysis of a volcanic ash tuff layer, only a few millimeters thick and discovered during excavations in 2024, revealed that the fossil-bearing Bromacker rocks are 294 million years old—four million years older than previously thought. These findings have now been published in the journal Gondwana Research.

science.title: How volcanic eruptions and internal climate cycles jointly shape Asian monsoon rainfall
science.summary: From the rice paddies of South Asia to the wheat fields of northern China, summer monsoon rains sustain the livelihoods of billions. Yet these vital rains fluctuate dramatically from decade to decade—a variability that has long puzzled climate scientists.

science.title: Dolphin mass strandings in Patagonia linked to killer whales
science.summary: In 2021 and 2023, hundreds of dolphins were stranded in shallow waters in San Antonio Bay in northern Patagonia. Some died, but many were returned safely to the sea. But what remained a mystery until now was how they ended up stuck on sandbanks in the first place. Now, a new study published in the journal Royal Society Open Science suggests that orcas may be to blame.

science.title: Photonic 'ski jumps' efficiently beam light into free space
science.summary: Photonic chips use light to process data instead of electricity, enabling faster communication speeds and greater bandwidth. Most of that light typically stays on the chip, trapped in optical wires, and is difficult to transmit to the outside world in an efficient manner.

science.title: Climate extremes hinder early development in young birds, research shows
science.summary: New research from the University of Oxford shows that cold snaps and heavy rain can stunt growth and reduce survival prospects in UK great tit nestlings. However, breeding earlier within a season appears to buffer against many of these weather-related effects. The research has been published in Global Change Biology.

science.title: Trouble swallowing? A nanogel tweak may keep therapeutic stem cells alive longer
science.summary: Swallowing is a fundamental human function that supports nutrition and communication. Damage to swallowing muscles can reduce quality of life and even lead to aspiration pneumonia or malnutrition. Many patients suffer from swallowing difficulties after being treated for head or neck cancer, and swallowing disorders are also common in older adults, yet effective therapies have been limited.

science.title: Despite their contrasting reputations, bonobos and chimpanzees show similar levels of aggression in zoos
science.summary: Chimpanzees have a reputation for being aggressive, while bonobos are often seen as their peaceful counterparts. This contrast has frequently been used to explain different sides of human nature. However, a new study by Utrecht University behavioral biologists Emile Bryon, Edwin van Leeuwen, Tom Roth and international colleagues shows that, in zoos, chimpanzees are not more aggressive than bonobos. The study was published in Science Advances.

science.title: Simple 'cocktail' of amino acids dramatically boosts power of mRNA therapies and CRISPR gene editing
science.summary: Lipid nanoparticles, or LNPs, best known as the delivery vehicle for the COVID-19 mRNA vaccines received by billions of people, are now at the center of a much larger medical revolution. Researchers are racing to use them to ferry therapeutic mRNA into cells for cancer therapies and treatments for inflammatory diseases, as well as delivering CRISPR constructs that can correct disease-causing gene mutations.

science.title: Childcare burden may explain US gender gap in poverty rates
science.summary: Gender differences in poverty rates in the United States may be associated with women's differing circumstances—particularly the burden of dependent children—rather than inherent to gender itself, according to a study published in PLOS One by Patti Fisher of Virginia Tech, U.S.

science.title: What primate faces reveal about empathy: Humans mirror emotions across species
science.summary: Humans perceive emotional expressions displayed by non-human primates and spontaneously mimic these expressions, according to a study published in the open-access journal PLOS One by Ursula Hess from Humboldt University of Berlin, Germany, and colleagues.

science.title: Biodegradable nanoparticles can seek and destroy diseased immune cells
science.summary: Johns Hopkins Medicine scientists say they have developed a simplified version of biodegradable nanoparticles that can "educate" the immune system to find and destroy disease-causing cells throughout the body. The study, they say, advances the field of engineering immune cells within a patient's own body to combat cancers and autoimmune diseases including lupus, among other conditions.

science.title: US data shows Arctic winter sea ice could break last year's record low
science.summary: Arctic sea ice is headed for one of its smallest winter peaks on record, an AFP review of U.S. data showed Wednesday, as climate change shrinks the region's frozen cover and heightens geopolitical tensions.

environment.title: Ocean temperatures may be protecting Earth from a planet-wide drought
environment.summary: Ocean temperatures may be quietly protecting the world from a global drought catastrophe. By analyzing more than a century of climate data, researchers discovered that droughts rarely spread across the planet at the same time, affecting only about 1.8%–6.5% of global land simultaneously—far less than earlier estimates. The reason lies largely in shifting ocean patterns such as El Niño and La Niña, which create a patchwork of drought conditions across continents instead of one massive worldwide dry spell.

environment.title: Scientists just looked inside Darwin’s 200-year-old specimen jars without opening them
environment.summary: Scientists have used a laser technique to analyze Charles Darwin’s original Galápagos specimens without opening their nearly 200-year-old jars. By shining light through the glass, the method reveals the chemical makeup of the preservation fluids inside. Researchers successfully identified the contents in most samples, offering new clues about historical preservation practices. The breakthrough could help museums protect millions of delicate specimens without risking damage.

environment.title: Blasted off Mars and still alive
environment.summary: A famously resilient bacterium may be tough enough to survive one of the most violent events imaginable on Mars. In laboratory experiments designed to mimic the crushing shock of a massive asteroid impact, researchers squeezed Deinococcus radiodurans between steel plates and blasted it with pressures reaching 3 GPa (30,000 times atmospheric pressure). Even under these extreme conditions, a significant portion of the microbes survived.

environment.title: The hidden technology that could unlock commercial fusion power
environment.summary: Fusion energy may be one of the most promising clean power sources of the future—but only if scientists can precisely measure the extreme, fast-moving plasmas that make it possible. A new U.S. Department of Energy–sponsored report urges major investment in advanced diagnostic tools—the high-tech “sensors” that track plasma temperature, density, and behavior inside fusion systems. Bringing together 70 experts from universities, national labs, and private industry, the workshop identified seven priority areas ranging from burning plasma to full-scale pilot plants.

environment.title: For every known vertebrate species, two more may be hiding in plain sight
environment.summary: Earth’s vertebrate diversity may be far richer than anyone realized. A sweeping analysis of more than 300 studies suggests that for every known fish, bird, reptile, amphibian, or mammal species, there are about two nearly identical “cryptic” species hiding in plain sight—genetically distinct but visually almost impossible to tell apart. Thanks to advances in DNA sequencing, scientists are uncovering these long-separated lineages, some evolving independently for over a million years.

environment.title: Ancient mystery on K’gari: World’s largest sand island lakes dried up during rainy era
environment.summary: K’gari’s iconic lakes have existed for tens of thousands of years—but they haven’t always been full. New research shows that about 7,500 years ago, during a time of high rainfall, several of the island’s deepest lakes mysteriously vanished. Scientists believe changing wind patterns may have redirected rain away from the island. As the climate shifts again, the lakes’ long-term survival is no longer guaranteed.

environment.title: Atacama surprise: The world’s driest desert is teeming with hidden life
environment.summary: Even in the ultra-dry Atacama Desert, tiny soil-dwelling nematodes are thriving in surprising diversity. Scientists found that biodiversity increases with moisture and altitude shapes which species survive. In the most extreme zones, many nematodes reproduce asexually — a possible survival advantage. The discovery suggests that life in arid regions may be far richer, and more fragile, than once believed.

environment.source: NASA Earth Observatory - Image of the Day
environment.title: Scoria Cones on Earth and Mars
environment.summary: The hill-shaped features are a sign of explosive volcanic activity—a rarity on the Red Planet. The post Scoria Cones on Earth and Mars appeared first on NASA Science .

environment.title: Massive asteroid impact 6.3 million years ago left giant glass field in Brazil
environment.summary: For the first time ever, scientists have uncovered a vast field of tektites in Brazil — mysterious glassy fragments forged when a powerful extraterrestrial object slammed into Earth about 6.3 million years ago. Named “geraisites” after Minas Gerais, where they were first found, these dark, aerodynamic droplets of natural glass stretch across more than 900 kilometers and may mark one of South America’s most significant ancient impact events.

environment.title: Jupiter’s moons may have formed with the ingredients for life
environment.summary: Jupiter’s icy moons may have been seeded with the chemical ingredients for life from the very beginning. An international team of scientists modeled how complex organic molecules—essential building blocks for biology—could have formed in the swirling disk of gas and dust around the young Sun and later been carried into Jupiter’s own moon-forming disk. Their results suggest that up to half of the icy material that built moons like Europa, Ganymede, and Callisto may have delivered freshly made organic compounds without being chemically destroyed.

environment.title: A major climate hope in Antarctica just melted away
environment.summary: A popular climate theory suggested that melting Antarctic glaciers would release iron into the ocean, sparking algae blooms that pull carbon dioxide from the air. New field data from West Antarctica reveal that meltwater provides far less iron than scientists once believed. Instead, most of the iron comes from deep ocean water and sediments, not from the melting ice itself. The discovery raises new questions about how Antarctica influences climate change.

environment.title: The first animals on Earth had no skeletons and that changes everything
environment.summary: Sponges may be ancient, but their timeline has been murky. New research suggests the earliest sponges were soft and skeleton-free, explaining why their fossils don’t appear until much later. By analyzing hundreds of genes and modeling how skeletons evolved, scientists found that mineralized spicules arose separately in different sponge lineages. The discovery rewrites the story of how the first reef-building animals—and possibly the first animals of all—emerged.

environment.title: Textbooks challenged by new discovery about how cells divide
environment.summary: Scientists have uncovered a surprising new way that giant embryonic cells divide—without relying on the classic “purse-string” ring long thought essential for splitting a cell in two. Studying zebrafish embryos, researchers found that instead of forming a fully closed contractile ring, cells use a clever “mechanical ratchet” system.

environment.title: Scientists discover microbe that breaks a fundamental rule of the genetic code
environment.summary: Scientists at UC Berkeley have discovered a microbe that bends one of biology’s most sacred rules. Instead of treating a specific three-letter DNA code as a clear “stop” signal, this methane-producing archaeon sometimes reads it as a green light—adding an unusual amino acid and continuing to build the protein. The result is a kind of genetic coin flip: two different proteins can emerge from the same code, influenced partly by environmental conditions.

environment.title: MIT study finds Earth’s first animals were likely ancient sea sponges
environment.summary: Scientists at MIT have found compelling chemical evidence that Earth’s earliest animals were likely ancient sea sponges. Hidden inside rocks over 541 million years old are rare molecular “fingerprints” that match compounds made by modern demosponges. After testing rocks, living sponges, and lab-made molecules, researchers confirmed the signals came from life — not geology. The discovery suggests sponges were thriving in the oceans well before most other animal groups appeared.

environment.title: Stunning 3D maps reveal DNA is structured before life “switches on”
environment.summary: For decades, scientists believed a fertilized egg’s DNA began as a shapeless mass, only organizing itself once the embryo switched on its genes. But new research reveals that the genome is already carefully arranged in three dimensions long before that critical activation step, known as Zygotic Genome Activation. Using a powerful new method called Pico-C, researchers captured this hidden DNA architecture in unprecedented detail, showing that a complex scaffold is built early to control which genes will later turn on.

environment.title: Scientists compared dinosaurs to mammals for decades but missed this key difference
environment.summary: Baby dinosaurs weren’t coddled like lion cubs or elephant calves—they were more like prehistoric latchkey kids. New research suggests that young dinosaurs quickly struck out on their own, forming kid-only groups and surviving without much parental help, while their massive parents lived entirely different lives. Because juveniles and adults ate different foods, faced different predators, and moved through different parts of the landscape, they may have functioned almost like separate species within the same ecosystem.

environment.title: Chesapeake Bay Locked in Ice
environment.summary: Nearly 50 years ago, the first Landsat satellite captured the rare sight of Mid-Atlantic waterways frozen over. The post Chesapeake Bay Locked in Ice appeared first on NASA Science .

environment.title: Antarctica just saw the fastest glacier collapse ever recorded
environment.summary: Antarctica’s Hektoria Glacier stunned scientists by retreating eight kilometers in just two months, with nearly half of it collapsing in record time. The rapid breakup was driven by a flat, underwater bedrock surface that allowed the glacier to suddenly float and fracture from below. Satellite and seismic data captured the dramatic chain reaction in near real time. The findings raise concerns that much larger glaciers could one day collapse just as quickly.

environment.title: Hidden architecture inside cellular droplets opens new targets for cancer and ALS
environment.summary: Biomolecular condensates were long believed to be simple liquid blobs inside cells. Researchers have now uncovered that some are actually supported by fine protein filaments forming an internal scaffold. When this structure is disrupted, cells fail to grow and divide properly. The discovery suggests scientists may one day design drugs that target condensate architecture to fight cancer and neurodegenerative disease.

environment.title: Ireland’s Old Irish Goat has survived 3,000 years
environment.summary: The Old Irish Goat isn’t just part of folklore — it’s genetically linked to goats that lived in Ireland 3,000 years ago. Scientists analyzed ancient remains and discovered that today’s rare breed shares its strongest DNA ties with Late Bronze Age animals. The finding suggests an unbroken Irish lineage stretching back millennia. It also adds urgency to protecting this critically endangered survivor of Ireland’s agricultural past.

environment.title: Lost fossils reveal sea monsters that took over after Earth’s greatest extinction
environment.summary: A lost cache of 250-million-year-old fossils from Australia has rewritten part of the story of life after Earth’s worst mass extinction. Instead of a single marine amphibian species, researchers uncovered evidence of a surprisingly diverse community of early ocean predators. One of these creatures had relatives stretching from the Arctic to Madagascar, showing that some of the first sea-going tetrapods spread across the globe with remarkable speed.

mix_items.title: Researchers show retinal images can accurately differentiate ALS and Alzheimer's
mix_items.summary: A retinal image could help doctors quickly distinguish between similar neurodegenerative diseases, such as ALS and Alzheimer's disease, and with remarkable accuracy, according to new research published in the journal Alzheimer's & Dementia. There is currently no objective diagnostic test for ALS or Frontotemporal Lobular Dementia (FTLD-TDP), in which the protein TDP-43 forms deposits in the spinal cord and brain, respectively.

mix_items.title: Golden lancehead genome reveals how genes responsible for venom toxins evolved
mix_items.summary: A research team led by scientists at the Butantan Institute in São Paulo, Brazil, has completed the most extensive genetic sequencing of a jararaca viper to date. The focus of the study was the genome of the golden lancehead (Bothrops insularis), particularly its venom genes. Since the species shares most of its genes with the other 48 species in the genus, the data serve as a reference for broader studies on the evolution of jararaca vipers and their toxins. The study is published in the journal Genome Biology and Evolution.

mix_items.title: Heavy water expands energy potential of carbon nanotube yarns
mix_items.summary: Researchers at The University of Texas at Dallas have developed a new electrolyte system that significantly boosts the energy-harvesting performance of twistrons, which are carbon nanotube yarns that generate electricity when repeatedly stretched. The findings could aid in the manufacturing of intelligent textiles, such as fabrics used to make spacesuits, that would power wearable electronic devices or sensors by harvesting energy from human motion.

mix_items.title: Entangled molecules can capture sulfate, offering future solutions for water purification and environmental monitoring
mix_items.summary: Researchers at the University of Jyväskylä (Finland) have developed a new class of synthetic molecules that can capture sulfate, a widespread industrial and environmental contaminant, with unprecedented efficiency in water. The study demonstrates that entangled molecular structures, long considered mainly chemical curiosities, can be deliberately engineered for real-world applications, including water purification, chemical sensing, and environmental monitoring. The study is published in the journal Chem.

mix_items.title: Lipsa resurselor continuă să blocheze accesul la educație pentru copiii din mediul rural. World Vision România și Citibank România dezvoltă programe educaționale pentru tinerii din mediile vulnerabile
mix_items.summary: București, 28 ianuarie 2026 – În multe sate din România, copiii cresc într-o realitate în care educația este un lux,... Articolul Lipsa resurselor continuă să blocheze accesul la educație pentru copiii din mediul rural. World Vision România și Citibank România dezvoltă programe educaționale pentru tinerii din mediile vulnerabile apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Fundația Regală Margareta a României continuă investiția strategică în educația intergenerațională și susține dezvoltarea a 6 centre sociale în anul 2026
mix_items.summary: proiecte de educație intergenerațională în 6 centre sociale de tip „școală după școală” din județele Alba, Cluj, Timiș și București 180 de copii beneficiază de sprijin educațional și activități de dezvoltare personală și vocațională minimum 50 de seniori sunt invitați să se implice ca voluntari... Post-ul Fundația Regală Margareta a României continuă investiția strategică în educația intergenerațională și susține dezvoltarea a 6 centre sociale în anul 2026 apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

mix_items.title: Titanium complexes cleanly edit the core skeleton of highly stable organic compounds
mix_items.summary: Multi-titanium hydrides can selectively snip the strong structural bonds of stable organic molecules called pyridines, RIKEN researchers have shown. This discovery could guide designing catalysts for applications in multiple branches of industrial chemistry, from oil refining to the synthesis of functional organic molecules. The findings are published in the Journal of the American Chemical Society.

mix_items.title: Is vaping more harmful than smoking cigarettes? More people incorrectly think so
mix_items.summary: A growing number of U.S. adults consider electronic cigarettes (e-cigarettes) more harmful than conventional cigarettes. The findings, by researchers from the UT Southwestern Medical Center and published in Nicotine and Tobacco Research, could have significant implications for public health policymakers, tobacco control strategies, and people who use either of these cigarette types.

mix_items.title: How climate-friendly is insect protein really?
mix_items.summary: The larvae of the black soldier fly (Hermetia illucens) are considered a promising source of sustainable protein, as they contain high-quality protein comparable to soy protein. They can produce this protein from a wide variety of biomass sources, including by-products and residues from agriculture and food processing. However, while their potential is the subject of intense debate, little is known about the emission of greenhouse gases during their rearing.

mix_items.title: In former college athletes, more concussions associated with worse brain health
mix_items.summary: In former college athletes, having had three or more concussions was associated with slightly worse physical, mental, behavioral and cognitive health five years after graduation, according to an article published March 11, 2026, in Neurology, the medical journal of the American Academy of Neurology.

mix_items.title: Neurologists issue guidance on the use of wearable devices
mix_items.summary: Wearable devices and digital apps that are available to consumers are emerging as potential tools in neurological care. These include smart watches and fitness trackers to monitor heart rate, physical activity, sleep, and other health factors. To provide neurologists with guidance on the use, limitations, and evidence on non-U.S. Food & Drug Administration (FDA)-cleared wearable technology, the American Academy of Neurology has developed an Emerging Issues in Neurology article published March 11, 2026, in Neurology.

mix_items.title: Why street layouts may matter for aging brains: Brain scans link walkability to memory
mix_items.summary: The simple act of crossing a road could help shield the brain from dementia and other cognitive conditions, according to new research from the Australian Catholic University and UNSW Sydney's Center for Healthy Brain Aging (CHeBA). The study found that people who lived in cities and frequently crossed intersections or relied on complex navigational skills had larger hippocampal tails.

mix_items.title: Compact vacuum ultraviolet laser may improve nanotechnology and power nuclear clocks
mix_items.summary: Physicists at the University of Colorado Boulder have demonstrated a new kind of vacuum ultraviolet laser that is 100 to 1,000 times more efficient than existing technologies of its kind. The researchers say the device could one day allow scientists to observe phenomena currently out of reach for even the most powerful microscopes—such as following fuel molecules in real time as they undergo combustion, spotting incredibly small defects in nanoelectronics and more.

mix_items.title: Galaxy-group motion suggests slower expansion in our cosmic neighborhood
mix_items.summary: Two new studies have measured the expansion of the universe in our immediate cosmic neighborhood using a novel method that analyzes the motion of two nearby galaxy groups within their surrounding cosmic flow. The results indicate that the local universe is expanding more slowly than previously estimated, bringing measurements of nearby galaxies into close agreement with observations of the early universe. The findings also suggest that less dark matter is required to explain the dynamics of galaxies within these groups than previously assumed.

mix_items.title: Astronomers collect rare evidence of two planets colliding
mix_items.summary: Anastasios (Andy) Tzanidakis was combing through old telescope data from 2020 when he found an otherwise boring star acting very strangely. The star, named Gaia20ehk, was about 11,000 light-years from Earth near the constellation Puppis. It was a stable "main sequence" star, much like our sun, which meant that it should emit steady, predictable light. Yet this star began to flicker wildly.

mix_items.title: New cancer therapies target epigenetic switch
mix_items.summary: Cancers emerge from many different paths. One path begins early, in embryonic development, when a protein complex called PRC2, which regulates cell differentiation, identity, and plasticity, becomes dysfunctional. PRC2 has well-established links to breast, prostate, blood, and skin cancers, among others.

mix_items.title: Friendly fungi hijack plant regulator to promote symbiosis
mix_items.summary: Plants are constantly on guard. Their roots are equipped with molecular alarm systems that detect invading microbes and trigger immune responses. Yet beneficial soil fungi routinely enter living root cells and establish close partnerships essential for plant nutrition. Scientists have now discovered that these fungi send small RNAs into the plant that may quietly switch off selected defense systems from within.

mix_items.title: One in three parents believe children always need antibiotics for ear infections
mix_items.summary: Over a third (36%) of surveyed parents believe children always need antibiotics for ear infections, according to new research appearing in BJGP Open. The study is titled "A survey of parental health-seeking behaviour, knowledge, and expectations around ear infection symptoms in children."

mix_items.title: New method reveals hidden stereochemical forms of oxidation in antibody drugs
mix_items.summary: Monoclonal antibodies are among the most widely used biologic medicines, with applications ranging from cancer treatment to autoimmune disease therapy. However, during manufacturing, storage, or transport, antibodies can undergo chemical changes that affect their stability and function. One common modification is oxidation of the amino acid methionine.

mix_items.title: Twisted bilayer photonic crystals dynamically tune light's handedness
mix_items.summary: Researchers at the Harvard John A. Paulson School of Engineering and Applied Sciences (SEAS) have created a chip-scale device that can dynamically control the "handedness" of light as it passes through—also known as its optical chirality—with a simple twist of two specially designed photonic crystals. The study is published in the journal Optica.

mix_items.title: People with HIV did not show more severe clinical symptoms during the 2022 mpox outbreak in Spain
mix_items.summary: People affected by human immunodeficiency virus (HIV), which causes AIDS, did not develop more severe forms of mpox than HIV-negative people during the multiregional outbreak of this disease that occurred in Spain in 2022. This is one of the main conclusions of an article published in Scientific Reports, which has analyzed 1,158 confirmed cases of mpox in adult men. The cases were reported between June 2022 and January 2023 in seven autonomous communities. The population sample analyzed in the study represents more than half of the cases recorded in Spain during this period.

mix_items.title: Robots vs. therapists: Live experiment tests AI's ability to give relationship advice
mix_items.summary: Can artificial intelligence offer meaningful relationship advice, or do human therapists still provide something machines cannot replicate? Researchers from the Max Planck Institute for Psycholinguistics recently explored this question during a public experiment at the InScience Film Festival in the city of Nijmegen.

mix_items.title: Bromacker deposit is 4 million years older than previously thought, new dating reveals
mix_items.summary: Analysis of a volcanic ash tuff layer, only a few millimeters thick and discovered during excavations in 2024, revealed that the fossil-bearing Bromacker rocks are 294 million years old—four million years older than previously thought. These findings have now been published in the journal Gondwana Research.

mix_items.title: Combining exercise and sleep coaching can help improve sleep and health markers in young women
mix_items.summary: Instances of insomnia are on the rise globally, which is a matter of great concern, as lack of sleep can take a heavy toll on one's physical and mental health. A recent study by The Education University of Hong Kong investigated how changes in daily habits, such as exercise and a sleep health intervention, can affect sleep duration and quality in adults under 30.

mix_items.title: Out-of-hospital cardiac arrest incidence rises on postholiday weekdays
mix_items.summary: In South Korea, postholiday weekdays are associated with a significantly increased incidence of out-of-hospital cardiac arrest, according to a study published online March 6 in JAMA Network Open.

mix_items.title: Promoting physical activity can be surprisingly inexpensive, yet impacts are rarely measured
mix_items.summary: A joint study by the University of Oulu and the Department of Sports and Exercise Medicine, Oulu Deaconess Institute Foundation highlights the need for better evaluation of both the effectiveness and the costs of interventions designed to increase physical activity. The study is an international systematic review examining the effectiveness and cost-effectiveness of population-level physical activity interventions.

mix_items.title: How volcanic eruptions and internal climate cycles jointly shape Asian monsoon rainfall
mix_items.summary: From the rice paddies of South Asia to the wheat fields of northern China, summer monsoon rains sustain the livelihoods of billions. Yet these vital rains fluctuate dramatically from decade to decade—a variability that has long puzzled climate scientists.

mix_items.title: Active ingredient of Viagra can help treat rare genetic disease
mix_items.summary: Sildenafil—an active ingredient also marketed under the name of Viagra—improves symptoms in patients with Leigh syndrome. This has now been reported in the journal Cell by researchers at Charité—Universitätsmedizin Berlin, together with teams from Heinrich Heine University Düsseldorf (HHU), University Hospital Düsseldorf (UKD), and the Fraunhofer Institute for Translational Medicine and Pharmacology ITMP in Hamburg. In a pilot study involving six patients, sildenafil showed a positive effect on the course of the disease.

mix_items.title: Dolphin mass strandings in Patagonia linked to killer whales
mix_items.summary: In 2021 and 2023, hundreds of dolphins were stranded in shallow waters in San Antonio Bay in northern Patagonia. Some died, but many were returned safely to the sea. But what remained a mystery until now was how they ended up stuck on sandbanks in the first place. Now, a new study published in the journal Royal Society Open Science suggests that orcas may be to blame.

mix_items.title: Photonic 'ski jumps' efficiently beam light into free space
mix_items.summary: Photonic chips use light to process data instead of electricity, enabling faster communication speeds and greater bandwidth. Most of that light typically stays on the chip, trapped in optical wires, and is difficult to transmit to the outside world in an efficient manner.

mix_items.title: FDA approves Sotyktu for adults with active psoriatic arthritis
mix_items.summary: The U.S. Food and Drug Administration has approved Sotyktu (deucravacitinib) for the treatment of adults with active psoriatic arthritis.

mix_items.title: Climate extremes hinder early development in young birds, research shows
mix_items.summary: New research from the University of Oxford shows that cold snaps and heavy rain can stunt growth and reduce survival prospects in UK great tit nestlings. However, breeding earlier within a season appears to buffer against many of these weather-related effects. The research has been published in Global Change Biology.

mix_items.title: Trouble swallowing? A nanogel tweak may keep therapeutic stem cells alive longer
mix_items.summary: Swallowing is a fundamental human function that supports nutrition and communication. Damage to swallowing muscles can reduce quality of life and even lead to aspiration pneumonia or malnutrition. Many patients suffer from swallowing difficulties after being treated for head or neck cancer, and swallowing disorders are also common in older adults, yet effective therapies have been limited.

mix_items.title: Epstein-Barr virus antibodies can distinguish MS from other neuroinflammatory diseases
mix_items.summary: The connection between multiple sclerosis and Epstein-Barr virus (EBV) is strengthening, according to a paper published this week in JAMA Neurology by a team of international researchers, including one from the University at Buffalo. Bianca Weinstock-Guttman, MD, SUNY Distinguished Professor in the Department of Neurology at the Jacobs School of Medicine and Biomedical Sciences at UB, a leading international expert on MS and the director of the New York State MS Consortium, is one of the co-authors of the paper titled "Epstein-Barr Virus Antibodies to Differentiate Multiple Sclerosis From Other Neuroinflammatory Diseases."

mix_items.title: Previously hidden immune circuit in the uterus sheds light on preeclampsia and early pregnancy failure
mix_items.summary: Early pregnancy depends on a remarkable act of coordination. Before the placenta can nourish a growing fetus, the embryo must securely "land" and connect with the mother's blood supply—a process guided by a specialized group of immune cells called uterine natural killer cells, or uNK cells.

mix_items.title: Despite their contrasting reputations, bonobos and chimpanzees show similar levels of aggression in zoos
mix_items.summary: Chimpanzees have a reputation for being aggressive, while bonobos are often seen as their peaceful counterparts. This contrast has frequently been used to explain different sides of human nature. However, a new study by Utrecht University behavioral biologists Emile Bryon, Edwin van Leeuwen, Tom Roth and international colleagues shows that, in zoos, chimpanzees are not more aggressive than bonobos. The study was published in Science Advances.

mix_items.title: Simple 'cocktail' of amino acids dramatically boosts power of mRNA therapies and CRISPR gene editing
mix_items.summary: Lipid nanoparticles, or LNPs, best known as the delivery vehicle for the COVID-19 mRNA vaccines received by billions of people, are now at the center of a much larger medical revolution. Researchers are racing to use them to ferry therapeutic mRNA into cells for cancer therapies and treatments for inflammatory diseases, as well as delivering CRISPR constructs that can correct disease-causing gene mutations.

mix_items.title: Childcare burden may explain US gender gap in poverty rates
mix_items.summary: Gender differences in poverty rates in the United States may be associated with women's differing circumstances—particularly the burden of dependent children—rather than inherent to gender itself, according to a study published in PLOS One by Patti Fisher of Virginia Tech, U.S.

mix_items.title: What primate faces reveal about empathy: Humans mirror emotions across species
mix_items.summary: Humans perceive emotional expressions displayed by non-human primates and spontaneously mimic these expressions, according to a study published in the open-access journal PLOS One by Ursula Hess from Humboldt University of Berlin, Germany, and colleagues.

mix_items.title: Biodegradable nanoparticles can seek and destroy diseased immune cells
mix_items.summary: Johns Hopkins Medicine scientists say they have developed a simplified version of biodegradable nanoparticles that can "educate" the immune system to find and destroy disease-causing cells throughout the body. The study, they say, advances the field of engineering immune cells within a patient's own body to combat cancers and autoimmune diseases including lupus, among other conditions.

mix_items.title: Medicinal cannabis eases endometriosis, pelvic pain
mix_items.summary: Medicinal cannabis could provide a new treatment option for those with endometriosis, with a study led by the University of Otago, Wellington showing it reduced pain, improved sleep and lowered anxiety. The study, led by Dr. Claire Henry from the Department of Surgery and Critical Care, involved 28 people with endometriosis and/or related pelvic pain who were prescribed cannabidiol (CBD) oil alone or in combination with dried cannabis flower for three months.

mix_items.title: US data shows Arctic winter sea ice could break last year's record low
mix_items.summary: Arctic sea ice is headed for one of its smallest winter peaks on record, an AFP review of U.S. data showed Wednesday, as climate change shrinks the region's frozen cover and heightens geopolitical tensions.

mix_items.title: Turning cancer's protein machinery against itself to boost immunity
mix_items.summary: A new study led by Pierre Close's team (GIGA, Laboratory of Cancer Signaling, and WELRI Investigator) reveals how subtly disrupting the way tumors produce their proteins can trigger a potent antitumor immune response. Researchers from the University of Liège and international collaborators have discovered an unexpected way to stimulate the immune system against cancer: by subtly disrupting how tumor cells manufacture their proteins.

mix_items.title: 16-year study finds major health burden in single‑ventricle heart disease
mix_items.summary: Children born with single-ventricle heart disease, a rare and serious heart defect, often undergo multiple surgeries in their first years of life. A new study shows the challenges for these children can last well into adolescence and sometimes throughout their lives.

mix_items.title: Orchestrated multi-agent AI systems outperform single agents in health care
mix_items.summary: As artificial intelligence (AI) becomes more common in health care, from managing records to assisting with medication decisions, researchers at the Icahn School of Medicine at Mount Sinai are asking an important question: How well does AI hold up when the workload gets intense at a health system scale?

mix_items.source: NASA Earth Observatory - Image of the Day
mix_items.title: Ailing “Megaberg” Sparks Surge of Microscopic Life
mix_items.summary: As Iceberg A-23A disintegrated, it shed meltwater that helped fuel an extensive phytoplankton bloom in the South Atlantic Ocean. The post Ailing “Megaberg” Sparks Surge of Microscopic Life appeared first on NASA Science .

mix_items.title: Ocean temperatures may be protecting Earth from a planet-wide drought
mix_items.summary: Ocean temperatures may be quietly protecting the world from a global drought catastrophe. By analyzing more than a century of climate data, researchers discovered that droughts rarely spread across the planet at the same time, affecting only about 1.8%–6.5% of global land simultaneously—far less than earlier estimates. The reason lies largely in shifting ocean patterns such as El Niño and La Niña, which create a patchwork of drought conditions across continents instead of one massive worldwide dry spell.

mix_items.title: Scientists just looked inside Darwin’s 200-year-old specimen jars without opening them
mix_items.summary: Scientists have used a laser technique to analyze Charles Darwin’s original Galápagos specimens without opening their nearly 200-year-old jars. By shining light through the glass, the method reveals the chemical makeup of the preservation fluids inside. Researchers successfully identified the contents in most samples, offering new clues about historical preservation practices. The breakthrough could help museums protect millions of delicate specimens without risking damage.

mix_items.source: NASA Earth Observatory - Image of the Day
mix_items.title: A Little Town with a Long Name
mix_items.summary: A NASA luminary from the Apollo era grew up in Wales near Llanfairpwllgwyngyllgogerychwyrndrobwllllantysiliogogogoch. The post A Little Town with a Long Name appeared first on NASA Science .

mix_items.source: NASA Earth Observatory - Image of the Day
mix_items.title: Searching for Selenite
mix_items.summary: Oklahoma’s Salt Plains National Wildlife Refuge attracts rare and diverse species—and enthusiasts looking for a distinct type of crystallized gypsum. The post Searching for Selenite appeared first on NASA Science .

mix_items.title: Blasted off Mars and still alive
mix_items.summary: A famously resilient bacterium may be tough enough to survive one of the most violent events imaginable on Mars. In laboratory experiments designed to mimic the crushing shock of a massive asteroid impact, researchers squeezed Deinococcus radiodurans between steel plates and blasted it with pressures reaching 3 GPa (30,000 times atmospheric pressure). Even under these extreme conditions, a significant portion of the microbes survived.

mix_items.title: The hidden technology that could unlock commercial fusion power
mix_items.summary: Fusion energy may be one of the most promising clean power sources of the future—but only if scientists can precisely measure the extreme, fast-moving plasmas that make it possible. A new U.S. Department of Energy–sponsored report urges major investment in advanced diagnostic tools—the high-tech “sensors” that track plasma temperature, density, and behavior inside fusion systems. Bringing together 70 experts from universities, national labs, and private industry, the workshop identified seven priority areas ranging from burning plasma to full-scale pilot plants.

mix_items.title: For every known vertebrate species, two more may be hiding in plain sight
mix_items.summary: Earth’s vertebrate diversity may be far richer than anyone realized. A sweeping analysis of more than 300 studies suggests that for every known fish, bird, reptile, amphibian, or mammal species, there are about two nearly identical “cryptic” species hiding in plain sight—genetically distinct but visually almost impossible to tell apart. Thanks to advances in DNA sequencing, scientists are uncovering these long-separated lineages, some evolving independently for over a million years.

mix_items.title: Ancient mystery on K’gari: World’s largest sand island lakes dried up during rainy era
mix_items.summary: K’gari’s iconic lakes have existed for tens of thousands of years—but they haven’t always been full. New research shows that about 7,500 years ago, during a time of high rainfall, several of the island’s deepest lakes mysteriously vanished. Scientists believe changing wind patterns may have redirected rain away from the island. As the climate shifts again, the lakes’ long-term survival is no longer guaranteed.

mix_items.title: Atacama surprise: The world’s driest desert is teeming with hidden life
mix_items.summary: Even in the ultra-dry Atacama Desert, tiny soil-dwelling nematodes are thriving in surprising diversity. Scientists found that biodiversity increases with moisture and altitude shapes which species survive. In the most extreme zones, many nematodes reproduce asexually — a possible survival advantage. The discovery suggests that life in arid regions may be far richer, and more fragile, than once believed.

mix_items.source: NASA Earth Observatory - Image of the Day
mix_items.title: Scoria Cones on Earth and Mars
mix_items.summary: The hill-shaped features are a sign of explosive volcanic activity—a rarity on the Red Planet. The post Scoria Cones on Earth and Mars appeared first on NASA Science .

mix_items.title: Massive asteroid impact 6.3 million years ago left giant glass field in Brazil
mix_items.summary: For the first time ever, scientists have uncovered a vast field of tektites in Brazil — mysterious glassy fragments forged when a powerful extraterrestrial object slammed into Earth about 6.3 million years ago. Named “geraisites” after Minas Gerais, where they were first found, these dark, aerodynamic droplets of natural glass stretch across more than 900 kilometers and may mark one of South America’s most significant ancient impact events.

mix_items.title: Jupiter’s moons may have formed with the ingredients for life
mix_items.summary: Jupiter’s icy moons may have been seeded with the chemical ingredients for life from the very beginning. An international team of scientists modeled how complex organic molecules—essential building blocks for biology—could have formed in the swirling disk of gas and dust around the young Sun and later been carried into Jupiter’s own moon-forming disk. Their results suggest that up to half of the icy material that built moons like Europa, Ganymede, and Callisto may have delivered freshly made organic compounds without being chemically destroyed.

mix_items.title: A major climate hope in Antarctica just melted away
mix_items.summary: A popular climate theory suggested that melting Antarctic glaciers would release iron into the ocean, sparking algae blooms that pull carbon dioxide from the air. New field data from West Antarctica reveal that meltwater provides far less iron than scientists once believed. Instead, most of the iron comes from deep ocean water and sediments, not from the melting ice itself. The discovery raises new questions about how Antarctica influences climate change.

mix_items.title: The first animals on Earth had no skeletons and that changes everything
mix_items.summary: Sponges may be ancient, but their timeline has been murky. New research suggests the earliest sponges were soft and skeleton-free, explaining why their fossils don’t appear until much later. By analyzing hundreds of genes and modeling how skeletons evolved, scientists found that mineralized spicules arose separately in different sponge lineages. The discovery rewrites the story of how the first reef-building animals—and possibly the first animals of all—emerged.

mix_items.title: Textbooks challenged by new discovery about how cells divide
mix_items.summary: Scientists have uncovered a surprising new way that giant embryonic cells divide—without relying on the classic “purse-string” ring long thought essential for splitting a cell in two. Studying zebrafish embryos, researchers found that instead of forming a fully closed contractile ring, cells use a clever “mechanical ratchet” system.

mix_items.title: Scientists discover microbe that breaks a fundamental rule of the genetic code
mix_items.summary: Scientists at UC Berkeley have discovered a microbe that bends one of biology’s most sacred rules. Instead of treating a specific three-letter DNA code as a clear “stop” signal, this methane-producing archaeon sometimes reads it as a green light—adding an unusual amino acid and continuing to build the protein. The result is a kind of genetic coin flip: two different proteins can emerge from the same code, influenced partly by environmental conditions.

mix_items.title: MIT study finds Earth’s first animals were likely ancient sea sponges
mix_items.summary: Scientists at MIT have found compelling chemical evidence that Earth’s earliest animals were likely ancient sea sponges. Hidden inside rocks over 541 million years old are rare molecular “fingerprints” that match compounds made by modern demosponges. After testing rocks, living sponges, and lab-made molecules, researchers confirmed the signals came from life — not geology. The discovery suggests sponges were thriving in the oceans well before most other animal groups appeared.

mix_items.title: Stunning 3D maps reveal DNA is structured before life “switches on”
mix_items.summary: For decades, scientists believed a fertilized egg’s DNA began as a shapeless mass, only organizing itself once the embryo switched on its genes. But new research reveals that the genome is already carefully arranged in three dimensions long before that critical activation step, known as Zygotic Genome Activation. Using a powerful new method called Pico-C, researchers captured this hidden DNA architecture in unprecedented detail, showing that a complex scaffold is built early to control which genes will later turn on.

mix_items.title: Scientists compared dinosaurs to mammals for decades but missed this key difference
mix_items.summary: Baby dinosaurs weren’t coddled like lion cubs or elephant calves—they were more like prehistoric latchkey kids. New research suggests that young dinosaurs quickly struck out on their own, forming kid-only groups and surviving without much parental help, while their massive parents lived entirely different lives. Because juveniles and adults ate different foods, faced different predators, and moved through different parts of the landscape, they may have functioned almost like separate species within the same ecosystem.

mix_items.source: NASA Earth Observatory - Image of the Day
mix_items.title: Chesapeake Bay Locked in Ice
mix_items.summary: Nearly 50 years ago, the first Landsat satellite captured the rare sight of Mid-Atlantic waterways frozen over. The post Chesapeake Bay Locked in Ice appeared first on NASA Science .

mix_items.title: Antarctica just saw the fastest glacier collapse ever recorded
mix_items.summary: Antarctica’s Hektoria Glacier stunned scientists by retreating eight kilometers in just two months, with nearly half of it collapsing in record time. The rapid breakup was driven by a flat, underwater bedrock surface that allowed the glacier to suddenly float and fracture from below. Satellite and seismic data captured the dramatic chain reaction in near real time. The findings raise concerns that much larger glaciers could one day collapse just as quickly.

mix_items.title: Hidden architecture inside cellular droplets opens new targets for cancer and ALS
mix_items.summary: Biomolecular condensates were long believed to be simple liquid blobs inside cells. Researchers have now uncovered that some are actually supported by fine protein filaments forming an internal scaffold. When this structure is disrupted, cells fail to grow and divide properly. The discovery suggests scientists may one day design drugs that target condensate architecture to fight cancer and neurodegenerative disease.

mix_items.title: Ireland’s Old Irish Goat has survived 3,000 years
mix_items.summary: The Old Irish Goat isn’t just part of folklore — it’s genetically linked to goats that lived in Ireland 3,000 years ago. Scientists analyzed ancient remains and discovered that today’s rare breed shares its strongest DNA ties with Late Bronze Age animals. The finding suggests an unbroken Irish lineage stretching back millennia. It also adds urgency to protecting this critically endangered survivor of Ireland’s agricultural past.

mix_items.title: Lost fossils reveal sea monsters that took over after Earth’s greatest extinction
mix_items.summary: A lost cache of 250-million-year-old fossils from Australia has rewritten part of the story of life after Earth’s worst mass extinction. Instead of a single marine amphibian species, researchers uncovered evidence of a surprisingly diverse community of early ocean predators. One of these creatures had relatives stretching from the Arctic to Madagascar, showing that some of the first sea-going tetrapods spread across the globe with remarkable speed.

joke_ro.text: Mi-am facut lista de treburi. Acum am doua: lista si vinovatia.

satire_ro.title: Unui român i s-a virusat buletinul electronic şi acum îl cheamă Măreşte-ţi Penisul Popescu
